tickets.title: Investigate and fix gateway restart loop (234 restarts in 24h)
tickets.notes: Root cause: (1) Node.js OOM crashes — gateway heap exhausted by 16MB sessions.json and 1.7GB sessions dir. Fix: Added NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 to LaunchAgent plist (takes effect on next restart). (2) False positive storm — obs-collector.sh timezone parsing bug caused same error log line to be re-logged every 5 min, inflating 8 real restarts to 235 obs events. Fix: patched line_epoch() to correctly parse +10:00 timezone offsets. Also cleaned 5 stale .tmp session files and reset lastRunEpoch. Actions: (1) Gateway currently stable pid 46502; (2) Schedule manual gateway restart or wait for next natural restart to pick up 4GB heap limit; (3) Real restarts were 8 OOM crashes over 5 days — acceptable once heap is increased.

tickets.title: US-FinOps: Deepen ROI data and justification for /finops report
US-AKB: AKB restructure, consolidation and migration — single source of truth for all knowledge
tickets.description: Knowledge is fragmented across too many locations: ~/.openclaw/workspace/ (MEMORY.md, RULES.md, state/, scripts/, memory/), ~/.openclaw/workspace-social/ (Spark), ~/.openclaw/workspace-business/ (Aria), ~/Documents/AInchors/ (Obsidian vault — Architecture.md, ModelStrategy.md, Decisions.md, etc.), plus Notion AKB Backlog. No clear ownership of what lives where. Hard to navigate, easy to miss updates. Objective: define a canonical structure — what belongs in Obsidian, what in workspace, what in Notion — and migrate accordingly. Every agent should know exactly where to read and write. No duplication, no ambiguity.
tickets.notes: Ken decision 2026-05-03 20:10 AEST: Obsidian → Notion migration. Retire Obsidian entirely. Notion becomes single KB. Notion needs full cleanup first — only AKB Backlog is live data, all other pages stale/orphaned. Sequence: (1) Audit all Notion pages, (2) Archive/delete orphaned pages, (3) Migrate Obsidian content into clean Notion structure, (4) Redirect AKB daily update cron from Obsidian writes to Notion writes, (5) Remove obsidian skill/dependency from all agents. | AKB renamed: Holocron. This US now covers migration of Obsidian + Notion cleanup into a clean Holocron structure within Nexus. Rename all references from 'AKB' to 'Holocron' as part of this work.

tickets.title: US-GOV: Deep review and strengthening of governance triad scope, skills and rules
tickets.description: Shield, Lex, and Sage were stood up quickly as part of the foundation build. Their current SOUL.md, RULES.md, and skill files are functional but shallow. Need a deep review of: (1) Scope — what each agent is actually responsible for, what falls through the cracks, overlap between the three. (2) Skills — what tools, scripts, and capabilities each agent should have. (3) Rules — are the current rules comprehensive enough for a production consulting platform? (4) Coverage gaps — content governance, client data handling, IP protection, AU compliance specifics. (5) Integration with Spark, Aria, and future agents — governance triad should gate ALL outbound content and client-facing work, not just ad-hoc requests. Outcome: hardened, production-grade governance with clear accountability and no gaps.

tickets.title: US-DR: Disaster Recovery strategy — multi-level outage playbooks beyond auto-heal
tickets.description: Current recovery only covers: gateway crashes (auto-heal), API key outage (fallback chain), config drift (auto-heal Check 12). No DR strategy for deeper failures. Need tiered playbooks: (L1) OpenClaw process crash — current auto-heal handles. (L2) OpenClaw total failure (corrupted install, failed update) — restore from config snapshot + clean reinstall procedure. (L3) Mac hardware failure or OS corruption — full platform restore on new or wiped machine, recover all agent state, keys, config. (L4) Catastrophic data loss — restore from backup. Options to evaluate and implement: (1) Container-restore — Dockerised OC1 image, portable and reproducible. (2) Hermes-restore — migrate from OpenClaw to Hermes as fallback runtime. (3) Offline backup-restore — encrypted state + config backup to NAS/external, restore script that rebuilds platform from scratch. Each playbook must define: trigger condition, estimated RTO, step-by-step procedure, responsible agent/person, test cadence. Integrate with OC2 HA architecture when it arrives.
tickets.notes: Additional recovery options added 2026-05-03: (4) External drive backup-restore — encrypted full-state backup to external drive (workspace, agent state, config, secrets export), restore script that rebuilds from drive on any machine. (5) openclaw reset recovery — explore openclaw reset as L2 recovery path: what state survives a reset, what must be re-seeded from backup, can workspace git repo + keychain export fully reconstruct a working platform post-reset? Define the minimum viable backup set for a clean reset-and-restore.

tickets.title: US-ARCH: Explore containerised OpenClaw — SaaS architecture with client, data and agent isolation
tickets.description: AInchors is moving toward delivering AI consulting services. Platform must eventually support multiple clients without cross-contamination. Explore containerised OpenClaw as the path to SaaS delivery. Key questions to answer: (1) Container model — Docker/Podman per-client container? Shared gateway, isolated agent workspaces? What does OpenClaw's container support actually provide today? (2) OC2 architecture — how do OC2-A and OC2-B (48GB M4 Pro, July 2026) fit into a containerised deployment? Can OC2 run per-client containers with local model inference? (3) Client isolation — data isolation (client data never crosses agent boundaries), access isolation (client can only see their own data), agent isolation (client agents run in separate containers/namespaces). (4) Interim architecture — what can be done on OC1 now before OC2 arrives? Namespace-based isolation? Separate agent workspaces per client? (5) Target architecture — what does a production SaaS deployment look like at 10 clients? 50 clients? (6) Security implications — S4 (least privilege), data sovereignty, AU compliance per client. Deliverable: architecture proposal with interim (OC1 now) and target (OC2 + containers) states. Ken decision gate before any implementation.

tickets.title: EPIC-NEXUS: AInchors Portal — Nexus (real-time Hive portal, API-first, Command Centre + multi-tenant client access)
tickets.description:
EPIC MASTER — links US36 (AInchors Hub) and US37 (Mission Control redesign) as child stories.

SYSTEM NAME: Nexus (proposed — Ken to confirm). Star Wars lore: a Force Nexus is a convergence point of power and information. Fits an API-first command hub perfectly.

TWO VIEWS:
(1) AInchors Command Centre — internal ops view. Real-time platform status, agent health, cost, task pipeline, sprint board, decisions log. Replaces current Mission Control static HTML. Live data feeds from OC Hive via API. Operators: Ken + Yoda.
(2) Client Portal — multi-tenant, abstracted view. Each client sees only their own data: project status, AI task outputs, reports, usage. Isolated per client. No cross-tenant data access. Access control: invite-only, token-scoped. Future: self-serve client onboarding.

API-FIRST ARCHITECTURE:
- OC Hive exposes a Nexus API layer — agents write structured output to the API, both views consume it
- All data in Notion (AKB Backlog already live) — Nexus reads from Notion as source of truth
- Real-time where possible (websocket or polling), near-real-time acceptable for MVP
- Runs on OC1 now, scales to OC2 containers when ready (link: TKT-0045)

LINKED STORIES (children of this EPIC):
- US36: AInchors Hub — real-time data feeds (Notion ID: 350c1829-53ff-8103-95b7-f52bffb560bf)
- US37: Mission Control redesign — Kanban format (Notion ID: 350c1829-53ff-8152-8a47-f759f46cd9a7)

DEPENDENCIES: TKT-0042 (Notion as single KB — must complete first), TKT-0045 (containerisation — informs client isolation approach)

DELIVERABLES: (1) System name confirmed, (2) Architecture proposal (interim OC1 + target OC2), (3) AInchors Command Centre v1 (replaces Mission Control), (4) Client Portal v1 (single client, manually provisioned), (5) API spec for Nexus layer.
tickets.notes: System name CONFIRMED: Nexus. Star Wars naming convention adopted for all modules. Holocron confirmed as the name for the AKB/Knowledge Base (TKT-0042). Future modules to follow Star Wars naming — see MEMORY.md for naming registry.

tickets.title: US-FABRIC: Explore Fabric framework — feasibility, patterns library, and integration with AInchors platform
tickets.description: Fabric (github.com/danielmiessler/fabric) is an open-source AI augmentation framework by Daniel Miessler. Core concept: a modular library of crowdsourced 'patterns' — reusable, battle-tested prompts for specific tasks (summarise, extract wisdom, analyse content, write, research, etc.). Works with any AI model. CLI-native. Explore feasibility and use for AInchors across three angles: (1) Internal agent augmentation — can Fabric patterns replace or enhance current agent prompts? Spark (content), governance triad (review patterns), Yoda (research/analysis). Reduces prompt engineering overhead. (2) Consulting tool — deploy Fabric as a client-facing productivity tool. Package curated patterns for AU business use cases: meeting summarisation, document analysis, decision frameworks, content creation. Adds immediate tangible value to consulting engagements. (3) Platform integration — can Fabric CLI be wired into OC1 as a tool for agents? Could Nexus/Holocron expose Fabric patterns to clients as part of the product offering? Deliverables: T2 research report on Fabric architecture + pattern library, feasibility assessment for each angle, recommendation on whether to adopt and how. Reference: https://deepwiki.com/danielmiessler/Fabric
tickets.notes: RESEARCH NOTES (2026-05-03): Fabric by Daniel Miessler — open-source framework for augmenting humans with AI. Core concept: modular 'patterns' — crowdsourced, reusable prompts for specific repeatable tasks. Each pattern = a markdown file with a carefully engineered system prompt. Works with any LLM (Claude, GPT, Ollama local). CLI-native (Go binary). Key capabilities: yt (YouTube transcript extraction + pattern), summarise, extract_wisdom, analyse_claims, write_essay, create_aphorisms, agility_story, explain_code, and 200+ community patterns. Architecture: patterns live in ~/.config/fabric/patterns/. Can pipe content in: 'cat doc.txt | fabric --pattern extract_wisdom'. Supports custom patterns — write your own. Active community (25k+ GitHub stars). Use cases relevant to AInchors: (1) Spark content pipeline — fabric --pattern create_social_media_post for LinkedIn drafts. (2) Governance triad — fabric --pattern analyze_claims for Shield/Lex/Sage reviews. (3) Consulting delivery — pattern packs for clients (meeting notes, document analysis, decision frameworks). (4) Holocron/KB updates — fabric --pattern summarize to auto-distil session transcripts. Local Ollama support = data-sovereign for client work. DeepWiki ref: https://deepwiki.com/danielmiessler/Fabric | GitHub: https://github.com/danielmiessler/fabric

tickets.title: US-DEV: Developer sub-agent(s) — containerised, trained, cloneable, team delivery framework
tickets.description: Build a developer sub-agent capability for AInchors. Not a single dev agent — a framework for spinning up, training, and cloning coding agents as a team. Key design goals: (1) Containerised — each dev agent runs in an isolated container. Code changes, dependencies, and side effects are sandboxed. No risk to production platform. Links to TKT-0045 (containerised OpenClaw architecture). (2) Trained — agents are pre-loaded with AInchors codebase context, conventions, RULES.md, relevant skill files, and project-specific instructions. Not generic coding agents — trained on our stack. (3) Cloneable — a base dev agent image that can be cloned per task/project. Spin up as many as needed, dispose after task. (4) Team delivery framework — orchestration layer (Yoda as lead) that assigns tasks to dev agents, tracks progress, reviews output, and merges. Modelled on a human dev team: lead + specialists. (5) Model exploration — evaluate qwen3.x (coding variant), Qwen2.5-Coder, deepseek-v3-coder, or other high-performance coding models. Target: best code quality at lowest cost, ideally local on OC2 for data sovereignty. Deliverables: (a) Model evaluation report (T2 research), (b) Base dev agent design (SOUL.md + RULES.md), (c) Container spec, (d) Team orchestration workflow, (e) First task end-to-end proof of concept.
